title: Flawed In-Order Traversal for Binary Tree Nodes with Multiple Children

statement.markdown: Given a binary tree, perform an in-order traversal on it. However, if a node has more than two children, only visit the leftmost and rightmost children. The function should return a list of visited node values.
statement.inputFormat: A binary tree represented as a node object with left and right child pointers.
statement.outputFormat: A list of node values in the order they were visited.
statement.notes: Note that this is a modified in-order traversal, where if a node has multiple children, only the leftmost and rightmost children are visited.

constraints.description: Number of nodes in the binary tree

examples.input: {value: 1, left: {value: 2, left: null, right: null}, right: {value: 3, left: null, right: {value: 4, left: null, right: null}}}
examples.explanation: The function should perform a modified in-order traversal on the binary tree, visiting the leftmost and rightmost children of nodes with multiple children.

hints.text: Start with the base case where a node has no children.

editorial.title: Modified In-Order Traversal
editorial.intuition: To solve this problem, you need to perform a modified in-order traversal on the binary tree.
editorial.bruteForce: Iterate over all nodes in the binary tree in-order and check if each node has multiple children.
editorial.betterSolution: Modify the in-order traversal algorithm to only visit the leftmost and rightmost children of nodes with multiple children.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a recursive approach to traverse the binary tree and use a stack to keep track of nodes to visit.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The modified in-order traversal algorithm visits the leftmost and rightmost children of nodes with multiple children, which satisfies the problem requirements.
editorial.dryRun: Perform a dry run on the example input to see how the modified in-order traversal algorithm works.
commonMistakes: Failing to modify the in-order traversal algorithm to account for nodes with multiple children.

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: std::vector<int> solve(TreeNode* root) { std::vector<int> result; if (root == nullptr) return result; if (root->left == nullptr && root->right == nullptr) return result; result.push_back(root->value); if (root->left != nullptr) result.push_back(root->left->value); if (root->right != nullptr) result.push_back(root->right->value); // TODO: handle nodes with multiple children return result; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: class Solution { public List<Integer> solve(TreeNode root) { List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>(); if (root == null) return result; if (root.left == null && root.right == null) return result; result.add(root.value); if (root.left != null) result.add(root.left.value); if (root.right != null) result.add(root.right.value); // TODO: handle nodes with multiple children return result; } }
